text: The surgical team performs a roboticohepaticojejunostomy for a common bile duct injury. Adhesions are taken down, and the ligament of Treitz is identified. The jejunum is divided 20 centimeters below the ligament, and the proximal jejunum is brought up through the transverse mesocolon. A jejunojejunostomy is created using a robotic stapler.
The surgeon closes the common enterotomy using a 4-0 V-Loc suture on a CV-23 needle, applying a continuous suturing technique.

The surgeon performs hilar dissection, mobilizing the duodenum and lowering the hilar plate. The gallbladder is identified and mobilized, achieving the critical view of safety before performing a cholecystectomy.

An intraoperative ultrasound is used to verify the placement of a stent in the common bile duct. The duct is opened, revealing the PTC stent at the bifurcation. The jejunum is brought up to the bifurcation, and a matching enterotomy is created. The opening in the duct measures approximately two centimeters, while a slightly smaller incision is made in the jejunum.

The surgeon constructs a diamond anastomosis starting at the six o'clock position in the duct, using a 4-0 V-lock suture. The anastomosis progresses symmetrically from both sides, with options for using different suture sizes based on duct size. The surgeon demonstrates various techniques, including tying, overlapping, and using a Weck clip, to complete the anastomosis.
The overlap of the anastomosis is visible as the resident ties the sutures, and a weck clip is placed. Gastric suspension is performed, creating a serosal patch to cover the anastomosis site.
